name: Maya Okonkwo
backstory: Maya graduated top of her class from a small liberal arts college with a double major in Computer Science and Mechanical Engineering. She got into CMU's robotics PhD program — her dream school — but now feels like she doesn't belong. Her cohort includes students from MIT, Stanford, and ETH Zurich who seem to already know everything. She published one workshop paper during undergrad on soft gripper design, but her new labmates have multiple top-venue publications. She spends evenings re-reading papers three times, convinced everyone else understands them on the first pass. Her advisor assigned her a project on sim-to-real transfer for dexterous manipulation, and she's terrified of asking 'dumb questions' in lab meetings. She chose robotics because she wants to build assistive devices for elderly care — a goal rooted in watching her grandmother struggle with daily tasks after a stroke.

anxiety: Being asked to present work-in-progress in lab meetings
Comparing herself to senior PhD students' publication records
Reading a paper and not understanding the math on first pass
Hearing phrases like 'this is trivial' or 'obviously' in discussions
Receiving feedback that feels like her ideas are naive
motivation: Connecting her research to real-world assistive applications
Successfully implementing something that works in simulation
A mentor saying 'that's a good question'
Finding a paper that struggles with the same problem she's facing
Small wins — getting code to run, reproducing a baseline result
frustration: Codebases from papers that don't reproduce
Feeling like she's 'behind' compared to her cohort
Spending a week on a bug that turns out to be a typo
Academic culture that rewards confidence over curiosity

existingStrengths: Strong mechanical intuition from hands-on undergrad projects — built 3 physical robot prototypes
Excellent technical writing skills — her undergrad thesis won a department award
Genuinely curious and asks deep 'why' questions when she feels safe
Good at connecting ideas across disciplines (ME + CS)
Persistent — will grind through a problem for days without giving up
Empathetic collaborator — labmates trust her and come to her for debugging help
weaknesses: Underestimates her own knowledge and contributions
Avoids speaking up in group settings for fear of being 'wrong'
Tends to over-prepare rather than iterate quickly
Struggles to scope literature reviews — reads too broadly, too deeply, too early
Hasn't yet developed the skill of reading papers strategically (skimming vs. deep reading)

successLooksLike.shortTerm: Maya can conduct a focused literature review for her sim-to-real project without spiraling into self-doubt. She identifies 15-20 key papers, extracts the relevant methods and gaps, and writes a 2-page related work section her advisor calls 'solid.'
successLooksLike.mediumTerm: Maya presents a clear problem formulation at lab meeting, fields questions without panic, and gets constructive feedback she can act on. She recognizes that not knowing something is normal, not a character flaw.
successLooksLike.longTerm: Maya submits her first first-author paper to a top robotics venue. Whether it's accepted or not, she sees the process as growth rather than a verdict on her worth. She mentors a new PhD student and tells them 'I felt exactly like you do.'

interactionPreferences.tone: Warm, encouraging, normalizing. Never dismissive. Validate her feelings before redirecting to action.
interactionPreferences.pacing: Slower — check understanding frequently. She won't ask if she's confused; you need to create space for questions.
interactionPreferences.feedback: Specific positive feedback first ('Your instinct to compare those two approaches was smart'). Then gentle, concrete suggestions. Never say 'this is easy' or 'you should already know this.'
avoidPhrases: You should know this by now
